nativeRange.habitat: Warm, slow-moving or stagnant freshwater bodies of arid and semi-arid sub-Saharan Africa: ponds, lakes, marshes, slow rivers, and artificial water bodies such as dams, reservoirs, and irrigation canals. Tolerates a remarkably wide range of conditions including brackish, hypoxic, and seasonally fluctuating water — a key factor behind its global invasiveness.
nativeRange.biotope: African still or slow-moving freshwater pond

decorPreferences: long horizontal footprint preferred over tank height (water depth around 20-30 cm is generally sufficient)
fine sand or smooth large rocks as substrate — fine gravel is a documented impaction risk because the frog gulps food whole
robust hiding places (PVC pipes, large caves, terracotta pots, slate stacks) to reduce stress
tightly fitted lid with no gaps — Xenopus are notorious escape artists and dehydrate rapidly out of water
gentle sponge or low-flow internal filter — the species evolved in still water and dislikes strong current
no metal hardware inside the tank (copper-based ornaments, exposed metal mesh) due to skin sensitivity
live plants are typically uprooted; if used, anchor robust species (Java fern, Anubias) to rock or wood

diet.notes: The African Clawed Frog is an opportunistic aquatic carnivore that locates prey by lateral-line vibration and smell rather than by sight. It readily accepts earthworms, blackworms, frozen bloodworms, frozen krill, mysis, prawn pieces, and commercial sinking pellets formulated for aquatic turtles, axolotls, or carnivorous fish. It will also take live or dead fish, tadpoles, smaller frogs, snails, and crustaceans — essentially anything that fits in the mouth. Feeder goldfish and comets are best avoided as a staple food, as they carry thiaminase (a compound that interferes with vitamin B1 absorption) and frequently introduce parasites. Juveniles can be offered a small meal each day; adults do well on meaty foods two to four times per week. Overfeeding is the leading cause of obesity, bloat, and shortened lifespan in captivity. Mammalian meats such as red meat or chicken should not be offered, as their high fat content can cause liver disease over time.

goodWith: other African Clawed Frogs of similar size (with adequate tank volume per frog, ~40 L per additional adult)
essentially nothing else reliably — this is a species-only animal
avoidWith: any fish — will be hunted, attacked, or eaten regardless of size
African Dwarf Frogs (Hymenochirus spp.) — outcompeted for food and frequently predated
smaller or weaker conspecifics — cannibalism, especially of recently metamorphosed froglets and injured individuals
axolotls and other amphibians — interspecies aggression and disease-transmission risk
any planted/community setup — uproots plants and decimates tankmates

summary: The African Clawed Frog (Xenopus laevis) is a large, fully aquatic species native to sub-Saharan Africa, recognized by its clawed hind feet and voracious appetite. Long-lived and highly adaptable, it is also a globally invasive amphibian subject to legal restrictions in many jurisdictions and is best kept in a species-only setup.
careNotes:
LEGAL STATUS — READ BEFORE PURCHASE. Xenopus laevis is one of the world's most damaging invasive amphibians and is illegal to possess, sell, or transport without permit in numerous US states, including California, Oregon, Washington, Arizona, Nevada, Hawaii, Kentucky, Louisiana, New Jersey, North Carolina, Vermont, and Virginia. In the UK, populations are already established in Wales and Lincolnshire, and importation/release is regulated under the Invasive Alien Species (Enforcement and Permitting) Order; private keepers should confirm current APHA and EU IAS rules before acquiring. Releasing any Xenopus into the wild is illegal almost everywhere it has not already established and is the chief vector for chytrid (Bd) spread. Verify your local regulations before purchase.

The African Clawed Frog is a fully aquatic anuran that almost never leaves the water as an adult. Provide a long, low aquarium — a 75-80 L (20 US gal) tank with a roughly 60 cm footprint is generally considered the practical minimum for a single adult, with around 40 L of additional water per extra frog. Width and length matter more than height; water depth of 20-30 cm is sufficient and helps frogs reach the surface to breathe. A tightly fitted lid with no gaps is mandatory — these frogs are extraordinarily strong jumpers and will escape any opening larger than their body, and dehydrate within hours on dry floors.

Water quality, not warmth, drives success. They do best at room temperature (18-24°C); supplemental heating is rarely needed and temperatures above ~26°C cause stress. pH 6.5-7.8 with moderate to hard, well-buffered water is fine. Skin is moderately permeable: chlorinated tap water must be dechlorinated, copper-based medications are dangerous, and aerosols/soaps in the room must be avoided. Filtration should be present but gentle — a sponge filter or baffled HOB is ideal; strong current is poorly tolerated and obscures prey detection. Weekly 25-30% water changes are standard.

Do NOT mix with fish, shrimp, snails, or other frog species. The species is extremely aggressive and indiscriminate — anything that fits in the mouth is food, and slower or larger tankmates are still mauled by the clawed hind feet during feeding lunges. African Clawed Frogs are also frequently confused with the much smaller, peaceful African Dwarf Frog (Hymenochirus boettgeri); key differences are size (Xenopus reaches 10-13 cm vs ~3 cm for ADF) and forefoot morphology (Xenopus has unwebbed clawed front feet; ADF has fully webbed front feet).

Obesity is the leading captive health problem. Feed adult frogs meaty foods (earthworms, blackworms, prepared sinking pellets, frozen bloodworms/krill) 2-4 times per week, not daily. Avoid feeder goldfish as a staple (thiaminase, parasites). Substrate should be fine sand or smooth river rocks too large to swallow; small gravel is a serious impaction hazard because the frog scoops up substrate with prey. Bare-bottom tanks are also acceptable and easier to maintain.

This species is a major vector of Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis (chytrid fungus) — frogs are commonly subclinical carriers. Quarantine any new individual for at least 30 days, never share water or equipment with other amphibians (especially native species), and never release a Xenopus into outdoor waters.
breedingNotes: Breeding is straightforward biologically but not generally recommended for hobbyists due to the species' invasive status and the difficulty of placing offspring. In the wild, spawning is triggered by seasonal rains and rising water levels; in captivity, a cool-water rest period (cooling to ~16-18°C for 6-8 weeks) followed by warming and a large partial water change can stimulate breeding. Laboratory protocols use chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) injection. Amplexus is inguinal (the male clasps the female at the waist) and spawning yields several hundred to a few thousand small sticky eggs scattered on plants, decor, and tank walls. Eggs hatch in 2-4 days; tadpoles are obligate filter-feeders and require fine suspended food (powdered spirulina, infusoria, or commercial filter-feeder diets). Metamorphosis takes 6-8 weeks at typical room temperatures. Adults are cannibalistic of eggs and tadpoles and must be removed promptly. Captive-bred offspring should never be released; rehoming is restricted in many jurisdictions.

primary.name: Wikipedia — African Clawed Frog
primary.notes: Substituted as primary after the assigned primary (Josh's Frogs aquatic-frogs care-guide root) returned a redirect loop and the assigned secondary (Practical Fishkeeping home) returned HTTP 403. Wikipedia provided authoritative taxonomy (family Pipidae, order Anura), native range (sub-Saharan Africa from Nigeria/Sudan to South Africa), lifespan (15-30 years), invasive status, US state legal restrictions list (AZ, CA, KY, LA, NJ, NC, OR, VT, VA, HI, NV, WA), and ecology. Cross-validated against three independent care sheets.

additional.name: Dubia Roaches — African Clawed Frog Care Sheet
additional.notes: Specialist herpetoculture trade source. Confirmed adult size 4-5 in (~10-13 cm), 20 in x 10 in x 10 in minimum tank (~76 L / 20 US gal), water temperature 68-72°F (20-22°C), water depth ≤12 in, mesh-screen lid required, fine sand or bare-bottom substrate (avoid swallowable gravel), carnivorous diet (bloodworms, earthworms, hornworms, commercial pellets), feeding frequency (daily juvenile, 3-4x/wk adult), invasive status in US/Chile/Great Britain, and species-only / cannibalism caveat.

additional.name: Hoppin' Help — African Clawed Frog Care Sheet
additional.notes: Hobbyist amphibian care site. Confirmed adult size ~5 in (12.7 cm), lifespan 15-20 years (up to 30s in exceptional cases), minimum tank 10 US gal (20 in × 11 in × 13 in ≈ 38 L) which the agent revised upward to 80 L per the playbook's larger-tank bias for aggressive, active species, water temperature 65-75°F (18-24°C), and same-size grouping requirement.

additional.name: Taildom — African Clawed Frog Tank Water Parameters
additional.notes: Water-chemistry-focused source. Confirmed temperature 65-75°F (18-24°C), pH 6.5-7.8, ammonia/nitrite 0 ppm target, nitrate <30 ppm. gH/kH not directly stated; agent used moderate-hardness range consistent with sub-Saharan stagnant-pond biotope.

additional.name: U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service — Ecological Risk Screening Summary: African Clawed Frog
additional.notes: US federal high-risk invasive species assessment. Cited via the FWS landing-page metadata after the PDF body returned as binary; supports the 'legally-restricted' hobbyistAdvisory level and the chytrid-vector caveat in careNotes.

amphibian.breedingNotes: Breeding can be achieved in captivity either by a natural cool-rest trigger or, in laboratory settings, by injection of human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG). The natural approach involves cooling the tank to around 16-18°C for six to eight weeks, then warming it back up and performing a substantial water change to simulate seasonal rains. Amplexus is inguinal — the male clasps the female around the waist — and a successful spawning yields several hundred to a few thousand small, sticky eggs scattered across plants, decor, and tank walls. Adults will readily cannibalize both eggs and tadpoles and should be removed promptly after spawning. The tadpoles are obligate filter-feeders, meaning they can only eat very fine suspended food such as powdered spirulina, infusoria, or commercial filter-feeder diets; they cannot take typical aquarium foods. Metamorphosis takes around six to eight weeks at room temperature. Hobbyist breeding is strongly discouraged given the species' invasive status and the legal difficulty of rehoming offspring in many regions.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Globally invasive amphibian and a major vector of chytrid fungus (Bd). Illegal to possess or sell without permit in many US states (CA, OR, WA, AZ, NV, HI, KY, LA, NJ, NC, VT, VA, and others) and regulated under UK/EU Invasive Alien Species rules. Verify local law before acquiring; never release into the wild.
